subtopics.subtopic_title: ONE-PAGE PRESCRIPTION TEMPLATE WITH INDIA-ADAPTED FOOD SWAPS
subtopics.category: CLINIC AND WARD IMPLEMENTATION CHECKLIST
subtopics.concept: A one-page prescription template tailored for paediatric patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) or chronic liver disease should be concise, clinic-ready, and include a reassessment trigger. On the same day as the prescription, it is essential to document the phenotype and stage of the disease, specifying CKD stage and modality or distinguishing cholestasis from cirrhosis with or without ascites. Anthropometry must be plotted on appropriate charts, such as WHO charts for children under five years or Revised IAP charts for older children, while noting whether weight is reliable or fluid-confounded, in which case MUAC (mid-upper arm circumference) or TSF (triceps skinfold thickness) should be prioritized. Decision-making should be guided by laboratory parameters, including sodium, potassium (##K+##), bicarbonate/total ##CO2##, phosphate, calcium, and PTH for CKD, and bilirubin, INR, and cautiously interpreted albumin for liver disease. Explicit nutritional targets, such as kcal/day or kcal/kg/day and grams of protein/kg/day, must be documented alongside the planned route of nutrition, starting with oral densification and escalating to enteral support if intake remains below 80% for two weeks. Restrictions should be framed as additive rules rather than bans to improve adherence. For example, cola-type drinks, seasoning packets, “lowsalt” potassium chloride substitutes, and packaged snacks containing phosphate or potassium additives should be avoided. Fluid restriction should be paired with a sodium plan on the same page, as sodium drives thirst. Binder timing should be documented for meals and snacks, along with the next planned monitoring date. India-adapted food swaps are critical for practical implementation. A school tiffin template may include one controlled-salt home item (e.g., poha, upma, or idli), one protein item (e.g., egg, paneer, or curd, adjusted per phosphate plan), one low-additive snack (e.g., roasted makhana without seasoning packets), and water. Fruit and vegetable portions should be adjusted based on dyskalemia status, but a blanket ban on produce should be avoided. Reassessment should occur within 2–4 weeks based on height velocity and MUAC trends, with early escalation if the nutritional trajectory worsens.

subtopics.subtopic_title: INDIA-SPECIFIC SUBSTITUTIONS WHEN RENAL OR HEPATIC FORMULAS ARE UNAVAILABLE
subtopics.category: CLINIC AND WARD IMPLEMENTATION CHECKLIST
subtopics.concept: When renal or hepatic disease-specific nutritional formulas are unavailable in India, substitutions must focus on three key goals: maintaining energy density without excess free water, ensuring adequate protein intake while minimizing exposure to phosphate additives, and providing micronutrients safely with appropriate monitoring. These principles should be explicitly stated in prescriptions to prevent families from resorting to dilution or restriction practices. For pediatric patients with chronic kidney disease, breast milk should be prioritized, with expressed milk tracking when feasible, or standard infant formula can be used. Energy density can be increased by adding measured amounts of carbohydrates or fats rather than increasing feed volume. In older infants and children, calorie boosters such as oil or ghee mixed into home-prepared foods are practical options. Regardless of age, stool patterns, vomiting, and growth should be reassessed every 2–4 weeks during nutritional adjustments. When fluid restriction is necessary, feeds should be concentrated, and sodium intake must be carefully coordinated, as excessive sodium can exacerbate thirst and hinder fluid management. In cases of cholestasis, if pharmaceutical-grade medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) oil is unaffordable, household oils can be used to increase total fat calories while ensuring sufficient essential fatty acid intake. Families should be counseled that coconut oil is not a complete substitute for MCT oil and does not eliminate the need for structured vitamin monitoring. Features of essential fatty acid deficiency (EFAD), such as dry, scaly dermatitis and poor growth, should be closely monitored when a significant portion of calories comes from functional fats. If water-miscible fat-soluble vitamin preparations are unavailable, standard formulations may be used, but absorption in cholestasis is less predictable, and both deficiency and toxicity risks must be considered. Vitamin dosing should be paired with scheduled laboratory reassessments and symptom reviews, avoiding indefinite continuation of high doses without monitoring. These strategies ensure safe and effective nutritional management tailored to the unique needs of pediatric patients with chronic renal or hepatic conditions in resource-limited settings.
